real_meaning: ～ように expresses a goal, wish, or desired outcome — it frames what you want to happen or what you're aiming toward, without directly commanding it. Unlike a direct imperative, it softens the intent into 'so that [this result comes about],' making it useful for prayers, requests, instructions, and self-directed goals. The speaker is pointing at a hoped-for state rather than ordering an action.

formation.rule: Attach ように to the dictionary form of a verb (potential or plain non-volitional) to express purpose or hope: <ruby>泳<rt>およ</rt></ruby>げるように (so that [I] can swim), <ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れないように (so as not to forget). For prayers/wishes, you often end with する・なる・<ruby>祈<rt>いの</rt></ruby>る etc.: <ruby>合格<rt>ごうかく</rt></ruby>できるようにする (work toward being able to pass).
formation.common_mistake: Using a volitional verb (intentional action verb) before ように when expressing purpose — that's the job of ために. Wrong: <ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>するように<ruby>頑張<rt>がんば</rt></ruby>る (awkward). Correct: <ruby>合格<rt>ごうかく</rt></ruby>できるように<ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>する. ように pairs best with potential verbs or non-volitional verbs; ために pairs with volitional action verbs.

sentences.jp: <ruby>試験<rt>しけん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>受<rt>う</rt></ruby>かるように、めちゃくちゃ<ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>してる。
sentences.translation: I'm studying like crazy so I can pass the exam.
sentences.notes: The potential verb <ruby>受<rt>う</rt></ruby>かる (to pass/be accepted) before ように is natural here — ように pairs well with outcomes you're working toward rather than directly causing.

sentences.jp: <ruby>風邪<rt>かぜ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>引<rt>ひ</rt></ruby>かないように、<ruby>手<rt>て</rt></ruby>をよく<ruby>洗<rt>あら</rt></ruby>ってください。
sentences.translation: Please wash your hands well so you don't catch a cold.
sentences.notes: ないように + ください is a very common pattern for giving considerate instructions or warnings — softer than a bare imperative.

sentences.jp: <ruby>全員<rt>ぜんいん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>安全<rt>あんぜん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>帰宅<rt>きたく</rt></ruby>できますように、<ruby>祈<rt>いの</rt></ruby>っております。
sentences.translation: We pray that everyone may return home safely.
sentences.notes: Ending a sentence with ように alone (or ように、<ruby>祈<rt>いの</rt></ruby>る) is the classic prayer/wish construction — the ように here hangs as a floating hope directed at the universe, not at a specific person.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>振<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>り<ruby>向<rt>む</rt></ruby>いてくれるように、いろいろ<ruby>頑張<rt>がんば</rt></ruby>ってみたけど、ダメだった。
sentences.translation: I tried all sorts of things hoping she'd turn around and notice me, but it didn't work out.
sentences.notes: This shows ように used for a wished-for outcome you can't directly control — the speaker can't force her feelings, so ように captures that helpless, hopeful aiming rather than a deliberate goal you execute yourself.

confused_with.contrast: Both express purpose, but they differ on the nature of the action and the degree of speaker control. ために follows volitional action verbs and describes a direct, intentional means to an end: you do X in order to achieve Y, and Y is a concrete goal you're actively pursuing. ように follows potential verbs or non-volitional verbs and describes working toward a state you can't guarantee — the outcome is hoped-for rather than engineered. Minimal pair: <ruby>医者<rt>いしゃ</rt></ruby>になるために<ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>する (I study in order to become a doctor — direct, volitional goal) vs. <ruby>医者<rt>いしゃ</rt></ruby>になれるように<ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>する (I study so that I might be able to become a doctor — framed as a hoped-for outcome, slightly less certain). Use ために when you're in direct control of both the action and the goal; use ように when the result depends on ability, circumstance, or external factors.

confusion_set.use_when: You want to express a hoped-for outcome or a goal you can't directly guarantee — especially with potential verbs or ないように warnings.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>字<rt>じ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>読<rt>よ</rt></ruby>めるように<ruby>練習<rt>れんしゅう</rt></ruby>している。(I'm practicing so I can read the characters.)

confusion_set.use_when: The goal is a deliberate, volitional objective and the means to reach it is a direct intentional action — full control on both sides.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>健康<rt>けんこう</rt></ruby>のために<ruby>毎日<rt>まいにち</rt></ruby><ruby>走<rt>はし</rt></ruby>っている。(I run every day for my health.)

confusion_set.use_when: You want to say you make a conscious effort or make it a habit/rule to bring about a certain state — adds the nuance of deliberate, ongoing effort.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>早<rt>はや</rt></ruby>く<ruby>寝<rt>ね</rt></ruby>るようにしている。(I make it a point to go to bed early.)

bunpro_tip: Mental model: imagine pointing at a floating bubble containing the result you want — ように is the act of pointing at that bubble and saying 'I'm working/hoping toward THAT.' When BunPro asks you to fill in a purpose clause, ask yourself: 'Am I directly causing this, or hoping for it?' If hoping/aiming, reach for ように; if directly engineering it, reach for ために.
